startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in Haematology. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Unexplained Fatigue

A 28-year-old vegetarian woman presents with progressive fatigue, pallor, and breathlessness on exertion for 3 months. She has heavy menstrual periods. On examination, she has koilonychia and angular stomatitis. Haemoglobin is 8.2 g/dL (normal for females: 12-15 g/dL).

Key concept: Iron is absorbed mainly in the duodenum and transported in blood bound to transferrin. Iron deficiency is the commonest cause of anaemia worldwide.

branchingQuestion.question: Given her diet and menstrual history, where is iron primarily absorbed and how is it transported in the blood?

alternatives.text: Iron is absorbed in the duodenum and transported bound to transferrin

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Iron absorption occurs in the duodenum, and transferrin is the plasma transport protein for iron.

alternatives.text: Iron is absorbed throughout the small intestine and transported by albumin

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Iron absorption is specifically localised to the duodenum, and it is transported by transferrin, not albumin.

alternatives.text: Iron is absorbed in the stomach and transported as free ferric ions

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The stomach acidifies iron to aid absorption, but actual absorption occurs in the duodenum. Iron never circulates freely — it binds transferrin.

params.text:
Investigation Results

Further investigations reveal: Serum ferritin 8 ng/mL (low), TIBC elevated, peripheral smear shows microcytic hypochromic red cells. Reticulocyte count is low. She is started on oral iron supplementation.

Concept: Erythropoiesis is stimulated by erythropoietin (EPO), produced mainly by the kidneys in response to hypoxia. The bone marrow requires adequate iron, vitamin B12, and folate for effective red cell production.

branchingQuestion.question: If this patient's anaemia worsened and she needed a blood transfusion, what must be checked first regarding ABO blood groups?

alternatives.text: ABO antigens on red blood cell surfaces and corresponding antibodies in plasma must be matched

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: ABO antigens are on the red cell surface and corresponding antibodies are in plasma. Mismatched transfusion causes haemolytic reaction.

alternatives.text: Only the Rh factor needs to be checked before transfusion

feedback.title: Incomplete
feedback.subtitle: Both ABO and Rh typing are essential. ABO mismatch causes the most severe acute haemolytic transfusion reactions.

alternatives.text: Blood group testing is unnecessary if haemoglobin is below 7 g/dL

feedback.title: Dangerous misconception
feedback.subtitle: Blood group compatibility must ALWAYS be confirmed before transfusion regardless of haemoglobin level. ABO mismatch can be fatal.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

After 3 months of oral iron therapy, her haemoglobin rises to 12.5 g/dL. She is counselled about dietary iron sources and menstrual management.

Learning points:

  • Normal Hb: Males 13-17 g/dL, Females 12-15 g/dL
  • Erythropoietin from kidneys stimulates erythropoiesis in response to hypoxia
  • The intrinsic pathway is measured by APTT; activated by contact with collagen
  • Iron is absorbed in the duodenum and transported by transferrin
